CS @ Caltech '26 · NYC/Pasadena · AI + Equity

Building to
include people
left out of the
digital world.

When technology fails to see people, I ask why. Then I build something different.

I want to understand how systems encode power: who gets counted, who owns their data, who gets to move through the world.

digital identity data encoding equity + tech civic infrastructure information ownership AI/ML
QR encoding ✦ digital ownership ✦ city as data ✦ identity + access ✦ counter-infrastructure ✦ information immutability ✦ who gets counted? ✦ QR encoding ✦ digital ownership ✦ city as data ✦ identity + access ✦ counter-infrastructure ✦ information immutability ✦ who gets counted? ✦
my thesis

Technology doesn't exist
outside power structures.

When we design systems without interrogating who they see and who they erase...we don't build tools. We build walls. I'm interested in what sits at the intersection of physical reality, digital infrastructure, and human belonging.

01 — The Physical ↔ Digital Boundary

How do systems translate bodies, places, and people into data? What's lost in that translation? What power do those encodings carry?

02 — Information Ownership & Immutability

Who controls data once it's created? How is it stored, modified, or deleted? Is our current setup inevitable, or simply profitable?

03 — Building Counter-Infrastructure

Technology itself is not the enemy. When existing systems fail people, we can build alternatives. I build tools that imagine different answers to "who gets access?" and "who gets seen?"

Physical
World
Digital
Systems
Human
Belonging
where I
work
the stakes

Inequity doesn't disappear
when it goes digital.

"The danger isn't AI itself — it's AI inheriting our biases, power dynamics, and assumptions and making them feel permanent, neutral, and inevitable."

Identity as a Gatekeeping Tool

Most systems assume you have a birth certificate, a phone, a bank account. What happens when you don't? Identity infrastructure excludes by design. How do we imagine a world without reliance on such techniques?

Cities as Learning Opportunities.

Transit routes, resource allocation, neighborhood boundaries — these aren't neutral choices. They encode decades of policy choices about who belongs where. Can we learn from history here to inform better choices about technological innovation?

Data Without Ownership

Who owns the information extracted from your behavior, your art, your body, your neighborhood? How do we restore ownership to whom we believe is the "rightful" owner? Do centralized models of info storage even accomodate for this?

work

Things I've
built.

01
Identity · Simulation

Untethered

A click-game simulation: you're born in a nation but have no birth certificate, no ID, no record. How do you pay? Travel? Access healthcare? A visceral look at identity infrastructure as a gatekeeping system.

in development · theme: who gets to exist, officially?
02
City · Visualization · Equity

Social Fabric NYC

An interactive visualization of New York City through overlapping lenses — inequity, human connection, transportation models. What does it mean to "map" a city? What gets emphasized, and what disappears? Each lens reveals a different city living underneath the same geography.

live · theme: whose city gets visualized?
03
Data Encoding · ML Research

QR Payload Extraction via CNN

How much information can be extracted from a QR code's visual structure without decoding it? CNN feature extraction reveals payload information that traditional decoding pipelines don't surface — raising questions about data, obfuscation, and assumed security.

research · theme: information exists beyond its container
04
Infrastructure · Independence

Mini Internet

A self-contained system with email, SMS, and a search engine — built to minimize big-tech dependencies. What does communication infrastructure look like when you try to own every layer of the stack?

in development · theme: what if we just... didn't use their pipes?
05
Spatial · Social

Rendez

An app to plan your night out with friends — at its core, a coordination tool for people who exist in physical space together. The physical world doesn't disappear when we have smartphones. Sometimes it just needs better scaffolding.

live · theme: irl community infrastructure
06
ML · Space Science

Cassini E-Ring Visualizer

Visualizing Saturn's E-ring using Cassini mission data. How do we represent astrophysical phenomena at a scale humans can understand? A question about the interface between data and perception.

completed · theme: making the invisible legible
07
Hardware · Wearable Tech

Ignitra

A wearable smart shoe system bridging the physical and digital — tracking motion, environment, and user data at the boundary between body and machine. Who owns what the shoe knows about you?

in development · theme: the body as data source
08
AI · Health · Speculative Design

Speculative Intervention

An AI/ML speculative design project: what happens when diagnostic tools are trained on unrepresentative data? Sits at the intersection of algorithmic bias, healthcare access, and the question of whose bodies get counted in the data.

in development · theme: whose body does medicine see?
✦

all of these projects seek to understand the limits of current technology for equity —
but are brave enough to imagine
creative alternative realities.

about me

Hey, I'm
Lauren.

I'm a CS + Econ student at Caltech (Pasadena, CA) from Massachusetts. Somehow, I always find myself gravitating back to NYC.


My interest in technology started with a question: why do design choices communicate so differently to different people? Coming from an AI/ML background, I noticed that the people building systems often couldn't see the systems they were creating.


I believe technology has the power to be a tool for creativity and beauty, but only if we build it with the intention of serving everyone. That means asking uncomfortable questions about who gets counted, who gets seen, and who gets to own their digital existence.
